original_abstract: Abstract Self-assembling molecular drugs combine the easy preparation typical of small-molecule chemotherapy and the tumour-targeting properties of drug–nanoparticle conjugates. However, they require a supramolecular interaction that survives the complex environment of a living animal. Here we report that the metallophilic interaction between cyclometalated palladium complexes generates supramolecular nanostructures in living mice that have a long circulation time (over 12 h) and efficient tumour accumulation rate (up to 10.2% of the injected dose per gram) in a skin melanoma tumour model. Green light activation leads to efficient tumour destruction due to the type I photodynamic effect generated by the self-assembled palladium complexes, as demonstrated in vitro by an up to 96-fold cytotoxicity increase upon irradiation. This work demonstrates that metallophilic interactions are well suited to generating stable supramolecular nanotherapeutics in vivo with exceptional tumour-targeting properties.
humanized_abstract: Metallophilic self-assembling molecular drugs This article has been selected for open access; Author(s): Nicolas Saint-Louisc ; Olivier Turlin ; Yohan Richard ; Yoan Cucurdeau ; Dominique Damour ; Florence Mollefaire ; Stéphanie Laurens-Foster ; Guillaume Morlot ; Sandrine Combes ; Cédric Brochier-Aitorrha ; Damien Thomas ; Aurélien Banquy ; Hugues Hombourger ; Aurélie Thibault-Crozet ; David Ray ; Marc Detalle ; Christophe Laprince ; Christine Dourthe-Nichterlein ; Hélène Allart ; Simon Hérynek ; Daniel Bénard Abstract Abstract Abstract Abstract Abstract Abstract Abstract Small-molecule chemotherapy has been a cornerstone in the treatment of cancers. However, their application is limited to relatively low doses on account of acute side effects. On the other hand, drug conjugation to targeted nanoparticles enables tumour accumulation of chemotherapy through passive targeting, and offers the possibility of increased drug dosage by enabling lower doses per drug to achieve enhanced antitumour effects. To develop an abstract self-assembling molecular drug the supramolecular entity that constitutes the nanotherapeutic must not rely on any stable interactions but rather make use of the variety of non-specific non-bonding short range forces arising at the interfaces between all species present in a living organism. To validate this concept we demonstrate that metallophilic interactions—originating from identical point charges on different metals—between cyclometalated palladium complexes generate high stability supramolecular nanostructures in a living mouse with a prolonged circulation time exceeding 12 hours post injection and tumour accumulation rates up to 10.2% injected dose per gram (IDG) in a melanoma skin tumour mouse model. Green light illumination of such supramolecular palladium nanostructures enables efficient tumour eradication through the type I photodynamic effect that is confirmed to reach up to a 96-fold increase of the cytotoxicity in vitro through dose-dependent photogenerated reactive oxygen species quantification. This communication paves the way to developing more straightforward approaches for the realisation of efficient tumour targeting supramolecular nanotherapeutics by making use of very common interactions for their assembly at the body scale.

This communication paves the way to developing more straightforward approaches for the realisation of efficient tumour targeting supramolecular nanotherapeutics by making use of very common interactions for their assembly at the body scale.
